video_title: The New U.S. Navy Shipbuilding Plan - Nuclear Battleships, FF(X) & America's Naval Buildup
transcript: For all the political polarisation in the States, there is something that both parties and successive governments have tended to agree on: world-leading navies generally benefit from having ships, and the United States Navy could probably do with more of them. To that end successive governments and naval leaders have released 30 year shipbuilding plans, containing new ideas and concepts of how to get the US surface fleet back on track. That said, for the last couple of decades the US has been much more consistent in producing those plans than it has the ships themselves. The LCS program was cancelled at a fraction of the intended program size. The Zumwalt destroyers were cancelled after three ships. The Constellation-class frigates after two. And more recently America's next-generation destroyer, the DDG(X), may have actually exceeded even the Constellation's performance by apparently managing to get itself sidelined before steel was cut on a single vessel. In place of the next-generation destroyer, the plan was now apparently the return of the battleship. And it is these that have made it into the most recent US shipbuilding plan, albeit with a twist. Because what was criticised by some as a gold-plated, overly expensive, industrially-challenging design has now apparently had the gold plating stripped off and replaced with platinum. Because USS Defiant apparently isn't just meant to be the first battleship built anywhere in something like three quarters of a century, it is meant to be the world's first ever nuclear-powered battleship, with a price tag to match. And so today we are going to crack open the recently released US Navy 30 year Shipbuilding Plan for fiscal year 2027, trying to get some insights into this new US naval build up, the vision for the future fleet, and the ongoing contest between things like the battleship and the budget. We'll start with a brief discussion of what this document actually is and, critically, what it isn't. Then we'll look at what kind of navy it envisions the United States building over the next three decades, splitting them into the big, the mostly unchanged compared to previous plans, the small, and the missing. That means looking at various ship classes, from converted Coastguard cutters turned frigates to nuclear-powered battle wagons, and asking how many the plan intends to build and how much it expects them to cost. Before finally zooming out and asking: in practical terms what does this thing actually mean for the future of US naval power? But first let me welcome back long-term sponsor of the channel, Private Internet Access. One of the oddities of the way the modern digital environment has evolved is that while sending data across physical borders almost anywhere in the world has never been quicker or easier, sometimes those physical borders have found a way to push back. In some places fragmentation of the internet is just a reality. And so if you want to navigate the modern digital environment while also trying to protect your privacy at the same time, you might want layers of tools and practices at your disposal. Just like a piece of military equipment might have a survivability onion made up of various active and passive layers, you as an individual might want to employ things like strong passwords, careful browsing, close control and custody of your physical devices, and also if you are like me, potentially a VPN. And as I have previously noted, unlike several of those streaming services, with PIA a single subscription allows you to cover an unlimited number of devices. Kind of handy if, like me, you have plenty of them on the go. So if you are interested, there will be a link in the description which give you access to an 83% discount on a 2 year subscription, plus 4 bonus months, covering an unlimited number of devices and advertised with a 30 day money-back guarantee. And so with my thanks to PIA as always, let's get back to it. OK, so let's quickly talk basics: the US military shipbuilding sector and where this plan fits into it. As we have previously discussed, US shipbuilding has a bit of a storied history. In the 2020s though, the days of Victory ship spam are long gone. The US now accounts for a fraction of a percent of global civilian ship building, leaving military ship building, such as it is, as a key bedrock for what US yards continue to do. The task of making bulk carriers or LNG tankers may have long passed to the likes of Japan, South Korea and China, but if you want the likes of a nuclear submarine or an aircraft carrier, a majority of the capacity is still in the States. And that makes the long-term shipbuilding plans of the US Navy and Coastguard pretty much the beginning and end of the story for large parts of the sector. And that is where the 30 year shipbuilding plan we want to discuss today comes in. Now let's be clear up front, that the plan isn't a statement of what is actually going to happen. Not just because things change, but because, critically, it is not an approved budget. In the United States it is the US Congress that ultimately controls the purse strings, and they get the final say about what ships are funded and potentially get built. Congress, year to year, ultimately has a huge amount of say over what the US Navy ends up looking like. They have currently legislated for example, a minimum number of carriers that the force needs to operate, and they have pretty consistently voted up more funds for shipbuilding than have been requested by the White House and the Navy. One of the best ways to think of the Shipbuilding Plan then, might be as a kind of vision or statement of intent for what kind of navy the Department of the Navy (so by extension the White House) wants to see built. It is a statement to the force, but also to Congress that this is basically the force we are trying to build, this is what we think we need in order to do it, this is how much we think it is likely to cost, so please go ahead and start coughing up the money on schedule. As well as qualitative comments about particular ship categories or the nature of the navy, one of the things we are really interested in here are two sets of data. You have detailed shipbuilding and funding requests covering the next 5 years, out to fiscal year 2031. So the future years' defence program, that is how many ships they want to procure in a given year and how much they are likely to cost. Then you have tables of force-planning information heading out 30 years, all the way out to fiscal year 2056. So how many ships are expected to be commissioned in a given year, how many are meant to be retired, and what will the fleet look like along the way? This plan doesn't tell us what kind of navy the United States will actually build, but it does tell us a lot about the navy the current White House wants to build. So before we jump into the detail: big picture level, what does the plan say? Like many of these recent shipbuilding plans, including the change-over in administration, this presents itself as a rebuilding plan for the United States Navy. Basic implications include the peace dividend navy being too small for current demands, US shipbuilding industry and the US Navy's record on ship procurement being a bit of a shambles, and there being a genuine need to grow the current battle force and expand the US shipbuilding sector significantly in the coming decades. To give you a sense for tone, the plan begins with the following, "We are at a strategic inflection point. For decades, while America's attention was elsewhere, our adversaries built fleets at a pace not seen in generations. During this time, our own maritime industrial base was weakened by inconsistent demand and misaligned priorities. This left our fleets smaller, our shipyards reduced in numbers and atrophied in capability and our people facing unacceptable risk. We will not allow these conditions to continue." Before you even get to the ships, it then sets out three pillars. That the Navy needs to shift from "a slow compliance-based bureaucracy to an accountable war-fighting enterprise." It talks about the need to build a larger, more lethal or more balanced fleet, delivering a high/low mix of advanced combatants, cost-effective frigates and unmanned systems, and also seeking to "ignite a renaissance in American shipbuilding." To do that, the plan calls for a significant increase in funding when you compare this to the fiscal year 2025 plan. Even if you are talking just about the next 5 years, you are looking at the shipbuilding investment budget for battle force ships jumping from sub-40 billion in fiscal 2025, to being north of 60 billion US dollars in fiscal 2027, 2028, 2030 and 2031. 2029 is a bit of a dip, but even then it is still close to $50 billion. Over the full 30 years of the plan, you are talking about a 12-digit difference. Expressed another way, over 30 years you might be looking at US military shipbuilding funding being topped up by more than the total value of all military aid to Ukraine by all parties since 2022. In terms of what that kind of funding is meant to buy you over time, what you are seeing here are charts using data taken from the plan, laying out some of the main components of the USN battle force by ship type between 2027 and 2056. As you can see, the goal here is to go from a sub-300 ship navy at present, to one pushing closer to 400 in the mid-2050s. Most of that growth though, is relatively slow to kick off and it is also unevenly distributed between ship classes. The carrier force for example, is meant to remain mostly static over the 30 years, and the combined cruiser and destroyer force is actually smaller in some of these forward estimates than it is currently. The growth in surface combatant numbers then, mostly comes from two types of ships. The guided missile battleships that start to appear in the force in the 2030s, and more importantly in raw numerical terms at least, an absolute spam of the new FF(X) frigates. For the nuclear submarine force the good news is that growth is likely to be significant, with the force going from just over 60 to more than 80 nuclear submarines of all types over the course of the plan. While the bad news being that it takes a considerable amount of time to get there. The force dips slightly in the early 2030s, starts to recover in the late 2030s and then seriously picks up from there. What these figures don't entirely capture is the changeover in type that is meant to happen behind those numbers, with the ballistic missile force entirely replacing the old Ohio with the new Colombias over the course of this plan. While one area that does see a rapid up-tick is in combat logistics and support vessels. This category, which includes a lot of critical ships like oilers, is meant to jump significantly between 2027 and 2037, with a lot of the growth being basically front-loaded and then stabilising in the 2040s. So basically across the board not many things go backwards. Some elements like support ships and nuclear attack submarines are scaled up, and two new ship classes are introduced at scale: the battleship and the frigate. Now it is hopefully obvious looking at charts like this one that the idea of this plan is to boost the size of the US Navy. The natural next question to ask then I think, is how much does it actually intend to do that, not just compared to the current baseline, but previous shipbuilding plans? So, first audience question of the episode: how many additional ships do you think all of that additional funding buys for the US Navy battle force over the course of the plan, compared to the fiscal 2025 version? OK, I am assuming your guesses are in. And if you picked any number that didn't start with a negative sign, you'd be wrong for most of the years covered by the plan. In ship count, but not necessarily tonnage, this plan would have the US Navy operating fewer battle force ships through most of the 2030s and all of the 2040s compared to the one from 2 years ago. Before eventually both the old and new plan converge on a battle force of about 400 ships in 2056. That is a significant increase in both cases from a force of 291 today. But it probably immediately raises the question: how do you plan to spend a shit ton more money and end up with fewer boats? The complex answer here is there are a lot of moving parts and factors pulling in multiple directions. The simpler, but still largely correct answer, is that the plan needs to build fewer ships because it plans to build some considerably more expensive ones. With the simplest answer being: in the brawl between the battleship and the budget, in this document the battleships are winning. Compared to the FY 2025 plan, the new one purchases fewer frigates, slightly more destroyers, and differences in terms of things like carriers and subs are mostly around the edges. The difference is that lone orange bar sitting by itself, the battleships. So, let's have a look at the ship and class that are apparently meant to be able to sink anything out there (including the budget), the USS Defiant and the ships to follow. We first talked about the US plan to bring back the battleship (or if you prefer, the heavy missile cruiser or the guided missile battle cruiser), soon after USS Defiant and the so-called Trump-class were first announced. This was a ship that was meant to put the "large" back in large surface combatant. A 35,000+ ton displacement ship mounting, among other things, a pair of forward-mounted conventional guns, a railgun, a standard VLS array, a hypersonic VLS array, as well as an assortment of directed energy weapons, which in most cases don't really exist yet. The apparent idea was to create something at the highest of the high end of the high/low spectrum. Something that didn't really have to make any design compromises on things like armament, sensors or aviation facilities, because it was big enough to just fit all of them. As we noted at the time though, the prospect of bringing back something with the size if not the displacement of the old Iowa-class battleships didn't come without its potential drawbacks. It was likely to be hideously expensive, there was a question over what US yards could actually build the things and on what timeline. The data released suggested they would be crew intensive. Some of the weapon systems they relied on were still thoroughly experimental at best, and there was even, just from a maths perspective, a question of whether or not the ship could efficiently do its primary job. As the current Acting Secretary of the Navy put it in their shipbuilding plan, the idea is for the battleship to "bring resilient command & control, deep magazines, diverse weapons, and long reach in one platform. Fires," he goes on to say, "is the king of battle, and wars are won by forces that bring both capacity and killing power to the fight - this ship delivers exactly that. " But as we have previously discussed, if you leave aside the experimental railgun for the moment and focus just on the missiles, the proposed guided missile battleship is kind of shit at carrying guided missiles. As you can see on this chart we showed last time, in terms of the number of Standard Missile vertical launch cells carried, the proposed Defiant is significantly out-gunned either by a pair of Zumwalts or a trio of either the US Arleigh Burke or the Chinese Type 55 missile cruiser. That matters because with a pair of Zumwalts or a trio of either other ship, you are looking at comparators that are either in the similar range or lower when it comes to both displacement or price when compared to Defiant. With either the very old now US Burke or the newer Chinese Type 55, you'd be looking at the Defiant being out shot on a missile-for-missile basis by something like 2:1 to 3:1, with tonnage and cost to spare. My point is there is a massive difference between a ship with a lot of firepower, and a force with a lot of firepower. And on a cost-for-cost or ton-for-ton basis, based on the numbers we have been given, the battleship is just a pretty terrible way to add additional missile firepower to a fleet compared to existing cruiser, destroyer and even some frigate designs. Now, sure, those ships don't have the larger VLS used for the Defiant's hypersonics, but the Zumwalts do. And those ships which, on paper here pack additional firepower, were, remember, considered overly expensive failures. But mere mathematical details like those aside, this plan doubles and triples down on the BBG (so guided missile battleship) concept, the basic idea here seems to be the battleship should provide the fleet with a higher end capability than a destroyer. It should provide, "longer endurance, higher speed, and accommodate advanced weapon systems required for modern warfare." The role of the battleship is thankfully not just to deliver high-volume long-range defensive fires, which is a good thing, given its lack of missile cells. And so some of the pitch here, if you like, focuses on the other traits that make the battleship different. In terms of those advantages, the document offers six hard arguments, if you like, and two soft ones. On the hard side, the battleship is meant to have room for advanced payload modules, including for future vertical launch systems with more capacity than the current Mark 41. Basically, eventually you might be able to fit bigger and more advanced missile systems onto it. It is also, point 2, meant to be a launch platform for theatre nuclear weapons. Which I take to be a suggestion that the US Navy's new nuclear cruise missile is meant to be mounted on this thing. Helpful perhaps from a survivability perspective, but it is also worth noting that in both cases there is no reason the battleship has to be the launch platform. You could put a nuclear cruise missile launcher on just about anything that floats, other countries already do. And if you go back to the Cold War, the US has tested firing missiles off some very small platforms in the past. The hypersonics also don't require something as big as a battleship to mount, the Zumwalts are already being fitted with them. The intention is to put them on submarines. And given their very, very long range, there is also always the option of just, you know, putting them on the back of a truck. Because, as the likes of Ukraine and Iran have helpfully demonstrated, while a modern warship might be pretty survivable, you can also do a lot with dirt, decoys and a cave or two. It is also meant to provide much more power generation capacity, more on that later. Advanced naval gunfire support (so think the forward mounted railgun), which the plan seems to envision potentially having an air defence role, not just ground strike. And then also the internal volume necessary to embark a fleet command staff. The final hard argument is this ship is meant to be so capable, so as to allow "the battleship to single-handedly perform key missions for the sustainment of commerce, like holding open choke points on critical sea lines of communication, that would otherwise require a wider commitment of assets from the fleet." Basically this thing is well-armed and well-defended enough that you could send it into the Strait of Hormuz alone, whereas normally you might be nervous about doing that. A counterpoint might be that it is reasonable to expect that a 35,000 ton ship would be able to do the job of multiple other ships, because it is a 35,000 ton ship. And for the same investment you could probably field multiple existing destroyers, and maybe a support ship or two. It is also hard to imagine one, or a handful of ships, escorting a meaningful number of vessels through a maritime choke point. While if you focus on the more coercive and strike-oriented side of the war in the Middle East, the carriers delivered a lot more firepower than a battleship would have been able to, while not needing to push into the Strait, and on a cost-for-cost basis other platforms would have had the battleship beat in terms of missile-slinging capacity. To cap it all off, the document puts forward what it describes as two unique advantages of the battleship. Firstly, that it represents a "commitment to the future." The basic argument here is the battleship will be large enough and have enough power generation on board that if in the future new technologies, new systems, new modules, new components are developed, there'll be plenty of room to integrate those onto the battleship and plenty of juice to power them. It may be over-engineered for current technology, but well suited for operations 10, 15 or 20 years down the track. The second argument is that the battleship provides "distinct enhancements to deterrence." "While unseen platforms like submarines and space systems can give adversaries pause by creating uncertainty, ensuring peace through strength ultimately requires presence, the arrival of a battleship, whether in the port of an ally or on the horizon of an enemy, sends an unmistakable signal of American resolve matched today only by our aircraft carriers." Basically while the battleship might be short on launch cells, it would be long on drip, an impressive way of showing the flag. And so one rocking up in an area might have deterrence value, you know, just like the arrival of Prince of Wales and Repulse in Singapore deterred the Japanese in the 1940s. So those are the arguments the document puts forward for the battleship, let's talk about how many they are planning to build. The plan calls for the first battleship of the class to be procured (so ordered, not commissioned) in fiscal year 2028, the second in 2030, then 2031, and then on a fairly regular cadence of one every 2 years. The idea is for the first of the battleships to enter service with the Navy in 2036, the second in 2038, finally reaching a peak of 11 of these ships in service in 2055. What that suggests is that come the 2050s, the US Navy would be operating battleships and carriers on a roughly 1:1 ratio. And that comparison might feel all the more apt when we start to talk about cost. When we first talked about USS Defiant and compared it to some of the other ships out there, the chart here shows the very rough numbers we used, about $9 billion a hull. That was a very rough figure based on tonnage, which suggested Defiant should cost more than three Arleigh Burkes, but a little bit less than two Zumwalts. Based on that figure, you'll remember the conclusion being the ship was probably too expensive at that price point relative to the capability it seemed to provide to merit large-scale inclusion in the force. If you were constrained on dollars, there might be more efficient ways to get the capability you seemed to want by comboing different platforms and equipment together. However that analysis assumed a 9 billion US dollar price point. And thanks to the Shipbuilding Plan, the US Department of the Navy has helpfully clarified they don't actually expect Defiant to cost $9 billion, they expect it to cost closer to $17 billion. With a billion dollars requested in fiscal year 2027, then $16.97 billion in fiscal 2028 for the first ship, with a total funding request out to 2031 of 43.5 billion US dollars (with a "B") for 3 ships. That gives you an average per unit price of about $14.5 billion, making each of these battleships as, or even more expensive, than a nuclear-powered aircraft carrier almost three times their displacement. And one of the many reasons for that incredible dose of sticker shock might be is that despite the labelling on the chart there, this isn't apparently a BBG anymore, it is BBGN. Because after repeatedly denying they were going to do so, apparently the Navy decided to take the battleship nuclear. Now this is interesting, because one of the last sections of my episode on the potential return of the battleship was a hypothetical. The basic question was, if you insisted on bringing back a battleship for a modern navy, what might you potentially do to make it useful? And one of the first things I suggested was that you use the size of the ship to fit a significant nuclear propulsion system. You'd do that because it would give you a lot more endurance, independence from logistics, and access to a lot more electrical generation capacity. Using that you could essentially turn your hypothetical battleship into a high energy platform, for lack of a better term. You could choose the most energy intensive over-the-top sensors, electronic warfare, and importantly: directed energy weapons (think lasers), and strap as many of them as you wanted to the platform. Because where a gas or diesel design might struggle, a reactor would manage. The original concept for Defiant and the Trump-class battleships though, was that they'd be CODAG vessels, COmbined Diesel And Gas. That potentially would have made them very, very thirsty, but also potentially saved a couple of bucks on building the things. As recently as a couple of months ago, the then Secretary of the Navy said that discussions were ongoing about potentially making the warships nuclear powered, but he said that was "unlikely." That was notably puzzling to many, including a number of Russian commentators, given that in the past when the Soviet Union faced a decision on how to build its own large high-speed guided missile ships, the Kirov-class battle cruisers, it had also come to the conclusion that if you wanted to build a high performance ship that large, you might as well stick a reactor in it, which they did. Now, leadership at the Navy Department has changed, and so too has the propulsion system for the battleship. It now seems the US is going to be getting its own Kirovs after all, With the USN's Chief of Operations publicly saying that the US Navy's decision to totally abandon nuclear power for surface combatants other than carriers, was "one of the largest mistakes it has ever made." He highlighted some of the difficulties the Navy had faced fuelling its conventionally powered ships taking part in operations against Iran. And through the discussions we found out that Defiant apparently won't just have a nuclear reactor, we also found out which one. Per the admiral, "We are going to use an existing design. We are going to use the Ford reactor, the A1B reactor, and the components that support that from the vessel, the steam generator, pressuriser, loop components, reactor coolant pump components. So all of that technology is going into the design of the battleship, the nuclear battleship, from the reactor plant perspective." Now, from a procurement and supply chain perspective, if you were going to make a nuclear powered battleship as the United States, I'd argue this is pretty close to the top of the list of how you'd do it. It takes a long, long time to design and stand up the supply chain for a new nuclear reactor type. Each design has to have its own supply chain, its own maintenance regime, its own training regime to make sure you have people qualified on it. And given how fiendishly difficult it normally is to obtain scale in the nuclear reactor business, using an existing design makes sense. And thankfully for the potential battleship, the reactor is a divisible asset. The 100,000 ton displacement carrier is powered by two of them. So hypothetically, you don't have to put the power plant designed for a 100,000 ton ship in the 35-40,000 tonner. You can chop the system down by half and be less ridiculously overbuilt. However, there is a difference between not being ridiculously overbuilt and not being overbuilt at all. You are still talking about putting 50,000 tons of displacement worth of power plant in a 35,000 to 40,000 ton vessel. And assuming the design and supporting systems can handle it, that leads to some pretty interesting implications for performance. It is generally a bit of an open secret that America's CVNs, and especially the Ford, are some of the fastest ships in the fleet. Officially, the Navy just says that the Nimitz and later Ford classes can do over 30 knots. How much more they don't say, but there is plenty of evidence to suggest that that "plus" is doing a lot of heavy lifting. They can also hypothetically maintain higher speeds for longer periods because they don't have to worry about emptying their bunkers the way something like a destroyer might. Assuming the battleship comes in at the higher end of its displacement estimate, so 40,000 tons not 35,000, on one A1B you are looking at a roughly 25% increase in power to weight ratio compared to the already outrageously fast carrier. Make some rough assumptions around other potential limitations like beam and water line, and you are inevitably going to come to the conclusion that unless there is some other aspect of the design holding it back, these BBGNs are going to be very, very fast ships. And as a side note, they may actually be in a better position to use that mobility strategically, operationally and tactically than the carriers are. The carriers are restrained by the fact that most of the time they have to be glued to their battle group. A carrier with its destroyers is a tool for power projection, a carrier out by itself is a very expensive target waiting for an ambitious submarine captain, air or missile attack. But if the US Navy genuinely believes the new battleships would be capable of independent operations, then there might be cases where they aren't glued to conventionally powered escorts. In which case, as long as you don't mind the potential quality of life and maintenance implications, they could potentially relocate around the globe faster than any other ship in US (or anyone else's) inventory. The numbers are also fairly confronting if you try and estimate electrical power. As a baseline, the US Navy's Flight III Arleigh Burke destroyers have gone through considerable upgrades and redesigns to get them to 12 megawatts of installed electrical power. By contrast, in the open source the A1B reactors on the Ford are capable of providing so much electrical power that the US is planning exercises where the Ford gets to play the role essentially of an offshore nuclear power plant for an emergency or disaster scenario. Practically speaking, you wouldn't really be energy constrained in terms of sensors, electronic warfare, and if in the future you successfully move from lasers in the hundreds of kilowatts range deep into the megawatts trying to provide air and fleet defence, not just close-in ship defence or dazzling, this is a platform that could potentially run them and run them continuously. You'd also have a lot of hull volume for things like cooling and just general space for systems and equipment growth. I think a likely scenario is that a ship with this much installed electrical power wouldn't be able to effectively use it all now. The energy-hungry systems that could require it don't really currently exist, but from a future-proofing perspective an A1B reactor would probably do it. There is also, as I said, more to like here from an industrial perspective about going with an existing design, the level of technical risk is reduced but not eliminated, you can use the existing training scheme and production line. Planning for maintenance, overhaul, modernisation and disposal is already in place. And so while (as the admiral suggested) it is likely to be a long way from plug-and-play, this is far more credible as an approach if you want to move quickly than trying to start with a clean sheet design. One way to characterise this change then would be that when Defiant was first announced, an accusation was that it was a gold-plated design. That it was at once both hideously expensive, but also didn't provide much in the way of unique capability (unless you are a massive railgun fanboy) to justify going with something new like this rather than cheaper existing concepts. The Navy's response to that criticism seems to have been to remove the gold plate from the design and replace it with platinum. Meaning you now have an even more hideously expensive design, but at least it can do some unique things. If built as described, it would be a very strategically mobile ship, it would have a degree of logistical independence, more firepower on a ship-for-ship basis than any other large surface combatant out there. And if you believe that, if not the fleet of the 2020s or 2030s, but the 2040s or 2050s might be in the laser business in a big way, this thing would have a significant advantage over conventional destroyers and cruisers when it came to the possibility of mounting a destructive disco of lasers, microwaves and everything in between. But (and there are a lot of "buts") that capability gain in swapping out the propulsion system, arguably makes many of the design's other issues worse. There were already concerns about where you'd be able to build these things and if you could do it on schedule, now you are limited to shipyards and workforces with nuclear capacity as well. It was already going to be an expensive design, and adding a nuclear reactor is probably unlikely to make it cheaper. The core point is that if this thing was actually built as advertised, then as the document seems to suggest, it probably would be quite a capable ship. It would bring a lot of performance, some nice-to-have features, and a lot of bells and whistles to the Navy's high/low mix. And I doubt a combatant commander that was told, "Hey, you are getting one of these things in your AO," would ever be disappointed having one available as compared to an Arleigh Burke destroyer. The challenge though is that when you are designing a fleet, resources are limited and everything is relative. And when you hypothetically have strategic competitors capable of turning out ships with fewer bells and whistles but almost equivalent missile firepower for a tenth of the price, there might be some pretty big questions to answer as to whether betting it all on the battleship is actually the best use of the available budget. Moving on from one of the big items that has changed compared to previous shipbuilding plans, I want to quickly talk about some of the things that haven't. Places where investment plans seem mostly consistent, with all the implications that has for future US naval power. The Ford-class carriers for example, are being examined for potential improvements. But for the moment the plan is apparently to keep churning them out. We found out that over the course of its most recent deployment, which kicked off in June 2025, Ford executed something like 10,500 fixed-wing launch and recoveries. With the report describing the class as now being a "battle-proven design." As we flagged in the force growth figures, there is also a lot of bubblehead in the budget as well, with the US due to spend more on nuclear submarines than it is on carriers, battleships, destroyers and frigates combined. Over the course of the 5 years, the US would order 10 Virginia-class attack submarines, with the per unit cost dropping from about $7 billion in fiscal year 2027 to about $5.6 billion in fiscal 2031, for an average of about $6.3 billion per unit over the 5 years. And as for the Colombia-class boomers, they appear to have kept their place at the top of the priority list. The idea seems to be to order one of these next generation ballistic missile submarines per year, for an average cost of about $12.4 billion per unit. That might seem ridiculously high, but it is in the same ballpark as the other next-generation boomers we know about in equivalent markets. The UK's Dreadnought for example, or France's new SSBN are both believed to be in roughly that category.
